narration_script: Iran has entered a state of survival following the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in a series of targeted airstrikes. The 86-year-old cleric was killed after decades of trading threats and missiles with Israel and the United States. Satellite imagery confirms the destruction of his secure compound in downtown Tehran, now reduced to a gray mass. While the strike marks the end of an era for a nation that has known only one leader for forty years, the Islamic Republic’s system of rule remains functional. Security has been tightened across major cities as volunteer paramilitaries and armed police patrol the streets to prevent potential uprisings. Despite calls from the West for a popular revolt, ordinary Iranians appear to be prioritizing safety over civil unrest. For now, a temporary council consisting of the heads of the judiciary, legislature, and executive will govern the country according to the constitution. Experts suggest the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps is poised to seize even greater political and economic influence during this transition. The leadership vacuum also raises urgent questions regarding Iran’s nuclear policy and its long-standing religious edict against weaponization. With Khamenei gone, internal voices seeking greater flexibility in the country's pursuit of deterrence may finally gain traction. Internationally, the assassination has drawn sharp condemnation from China and Russia, while the region braces for the seismic consequences of this power shift. Khamenei’s legacy will ultimately be defined by his iron fist and a four-decade fixation on challenging Western interests.

key_points.label: Khamenei Killed in Airstrikes
key_points.excerpt: The 86-year-old cleric was killed after decades of trading threats and, more recently, missiles with Israel and the US.
key_points.script_anchor: The 86-year-old cleric was killed after decades of trading threats and missiles with Israel and the United States.

key_points.label: Tehran Compound Destroyed
key_points.excerpt: Satellite images showed the secure compound in downtown Tehran where his residence and offices had been, reduced to a gray mass.

key_points.label: Security Forces Tighten Control
key_points.excerpt: Security in towns and cities across the country has been tightened with reports from residents in the capital Tehran of large numbers of volunteer paramilitaries and armed police in the streets.
key_points.script_anchor: volunteer paramilitaries and armed police patrol the streets

key_points.label: Constitutional Council Takes Power
key_points.excerpt: In the short term, Iran will be governed according to the constitution. It will be governed by a council made up of the heads of the judiciary, the legislature, and the executive,
key_points.script_anchor: council consisting of the heads of the judiciary, legislature, and executive

key_points.label: IRGC Influence Expected to Grow
key_points.excerpt: it's likely that the IRGC - the powerful wing of Iran's armed forces originally designed to protect the regime and its supreme leader - will assume much more power
key_points.script_anchor: Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps is poised to seize even greater political and economic influence

key_points.label: Nuclear Doctrine Shift Possible
key_points.excerpt: With Khamenei's demise, voices that have sought greater flexibility in Iran's pursuit of deterrence may gain new strength.
key_points.script_anchor: voices seeking greater flexibility in the country's pursuit of deterrence

key_points.label: Legacy of Brutality
key_points.excerpt: "Khamenei's legacy will be one of brutality," said Dina Esfandiary, geo-economics analyst at Bloomberg Economics, "people will remember his iron fist in crushing dissent."
key_points.script_anchor: iron fist and a four-decade fixation on challenging Western interests.
